title: S.M. v. Croatia [2020] ECHR (Grand Chamber) 60561/14
issuing_body: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber)
summary: Grand Chamber judgment clarifying that forced prostitution and sexual exploitation fall within the scope of Article 4's prohibition on trafficking. The Court confirmed that Art. 4 encompasses trafficking in human beings and that states have procedural obligations to investigate credible allegations of trafficking for sexual exploitation, reinforcing the duty of authorities to identify potential victims at the investigative stage.
key_pinpoint: Paras. 290–313 (Art. 4 scope covers trafficking and forced prostitution; procedural obligation to investigate)

title: Zoletic and Others v Azerbaijan, App. No. 20116/12 (ECtHR, 2021)
summary: ECtHR found Azerbaijan violated Article 4 in a case involving Bosnian construction workers subjected to forced labour. Workers had passports confiscated, were unpaid, and lived in degrading conditions. Court confirmed that states must have operational measures to protect victims and investigate forced labour.
key_pinpoint: Paras 140-160 (forced labour indicators — passport confiscation, non-payment, degrading conditions), paras 161-180 (procedural obligation)

title: Prosecutor v Kunarac, Kovac and Vukovic, IT-96-23 & IT-96-23/1-A (ICTY, 2001)
issuing_body: International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia
summary: ICTY Appeals Chamber landmark on enslavement as a crime against humanity. Defined enslavement broadly to include modern forms of slavery — trafficking for sexual exploitation, forced labour, and exercise of powers attaching to right of ownership. Key international law authority on the elements of enslavement.
key_pinpoint: Paras 116-124 (definition of enslavement), paras 125-130 (indicators of enslavement including control of movement, psychological control, forced labour)

title: Prosecutor v Bosco Ntaganda, ICC-01/04-02/06 (ICC, 2019)
summary: ICC Trial Chamber convicted Ntaganda of sexual slavery and forced marriage as crimes against humanity and war crimes. Held that sexual slavery constitutes a form of enslavement involving sexual violence, and that the perpetrator need not have legal ownership over the victim.
key_pinpoint: Paras 950-975 (elements of sexual slavery), paras 976-990 (forced marriage as other inhumane act)

title: Prosecutor v Dominic Ongwen, ICC-02/04-01/15 (ICC, 2021)
summary: ICC Trial Chamber convicted Ongwen of sexual slavery, forced marriage, and forced pregnancy as crimes against humanity. Significant for recognising forced marriage as a distinct crime and for extensive findings on psychological coercion in the context of enslavement.
key_pinpoint: Paras 2740-2780 (sexual slavery elements), paras 2990-3020 (forced marriage as separate crime), paras on psychological coercion

title: Bandhua Mukti Morcha v Union of India, AIR 1984 SC 802
summary: LANDMARK Indian SC PIL case. Held: bonded labour includes any forced labour from economic compulsion incl. debt bondage without formal agreements; onus on employer to disprove; State must proactively identify, release, rehabilitate. Expanded Art. 21 to include dignity, minimum wages, shelter.
key_pinpoint: Art. 21 includes socioeconomic minima; Art. 23 directly enforceable; State's affirmative duty; PIL standing for NGOs established

title: Sanjit Roy v State of Rajasthan, AIR 1983 SC 328
summary: Supreme Court held that paying wages below the minimum wage under famine relief works constitutes 'forced labour' prohibited under Article 23 of the Constitution. Established that economic compulsion can vitiate consent to work, making it forced labour even absent physical coercion.
key_pinpoint: Paras 10-15 (economic compulsion as forced labour), holding on Art. 23

title: Vishal Jeet v Union of India, AIR 1990 SC 1412
summary: PIL petition on the exploitation of women and children in prostitution. Supreme Court issued directions to state governments for rescue and rehabilitation of sex trafficking victims, setting up advisory committees. Early authority recognising state obligations to combat trafficking under Article 23.
key_pinpoint: Directions to states (rescue operations, advisory committees, rehabilitation)

title: Basfar v Wong [2022] UKSC 20
summary: Supreme Court held that a victim of domestic servitude could bring a civil claim against her employer/trafficker under the Modern Slavery Act 2015 s.8, even where the exploitation occurred before the Act came into force. Clarified the definition of servitude and forced labour under Article 4 ECHR as incorporated into domestic law.
key_pinpoint: Paras 1-5 (definition of servitude), paras 25-40 (civil remedy under MSA s.8)

title: MK v R [2018] EWCA Crim 667
summary: Court of Appeal considered whether a trafficking victim who committed criminal offences under compulsion could rely on the s.45 Modern Slavery Act 2015 defence. Clarified the relationship between the statutory defence and the common law defence of duress, and the evidential threshold for raising s.45.
key_pinpoint: Paras 20-35 (s.45 defence scope), paras 36-42 (relationship with duress)

title: R v AAD, AAH, AAI [2022] EWCA Crim 106
summary: Essential CoA guidance. Restores abuse of process jurisdiction (overturning DS). SCA decisions inadmissible before jury (Brecani upheld) BUT admissible on appeal and in abuse of process. Principle of finality does not apply in guilty plea cases where defendant is trafficking victim. Found Art. 4 breaches where no proper investigation occurred.
key_pinpoint: ¶86–87 (abuse of process restored); ¶141 (DS was wrong); ¶108 (calling defendant as witness); interplay NRM/CPS/criminal proceedings

title: R v Brecani [2021] EWCA Crim 731
summary: Held SCA conclusive grounds decisions INADMISSIBLE before a jury. Question of victim status is for the jury under §45. Criticised but upheld in AAD (limited to trial; admissible on appeal).
key_pinpoint: Admissibility of SCA decisions; relationship NRM and criminal proceedings; confirmed in AAD at ¶81–85

title: R v. DS [2020] EWCA Crim 285
summary: Court of Appeal examined the application of the s. 45 Modern Slavery Act defence and the relationship between victim identification and non-punishment. Held that early identification of trafficking status is of paramount importance, and that a credible suspicion of trafficking must trigger a qualified assessment before any prosecution decision. The jury, not the judge on abuse of process, determines trafficking facts.
key_pinpoint: Paras. on s. 45 defence application, abuse of process arguments, and the nexus between victim identification and non-prosecution

title: R v Joseph & Others [2017] EWCA Crim 36
summary: Landmark appeal on conspiracy to traffic for labour exploitation. Court of Appeal clarified the meaning of 'exploitation' under MSA 2015 s.3, holding that it encompasses a broad range of conduct and the jury must assess whether the arrangement amounted to exploitation in context.
key_pinpoint: Paras 28-45 (meaning of exploitation under s.3 MSA 2015), para 52 (sufficiency of evidence)

title: R v N; R v L [2019] EWCA Crim 984
summary: Key authority on the s.45 MSA 2015 defence. Court of Appeal held that the defence requires a nexus between the trafficking and the criminal offence, and that the reasonable person test must account for the victim's circumstances including age, trauma, and control by traffickers.
key_pinpoint: Paras 35-50 (nexus requirement), paras 51-60 (reasonable person test)

title: VCL and AN v. United Kingdom [2021] ECHR 77587/12
summary: The ECtHR held that the UK violated Article 4 ECHR by failing to identify two Vietnamese children as victims of trafficking before prosecuting them for cannabis cultivation offences. Establishes that once authorities are aware of a credible suspicion of trafficking, the individual must be assessed by a qualified person before any prosecution decision is taken.
key_pinpoint: Paras. 153–162 (positive obligation to identify victims), paras. 192–200 (Article 6 fair trial implications of non-identification)

title: United States v. Askarkhodjaev, No. 09-00191 (W.D. Mo. 2011)
issuing_body: US District Court, Western District of Missouri
retrieval_notes: Case No. 4:09-cr-00191 (W.D. Mo.). Search PACER for US v. Askarkhodjaev. Large-scale labour trafficking conspiracy.
summary: Large-scale labour trafficking prosecution involving recruitment of Uzbek and Kazakh nationals for hospitality work in the US. Defendants used fraud, debt bondage, and document confiscation. Significant case on the element of fraud in recruitment as an act of trafficking.
key_pinpoint: Indictment and plea agreement (fraud in recruitment, debt bondage mechanisms)

title: United States v. Calimlim, 538 F.3d 706 (7th Cir. 2008)
issuing_body: US Court of Appeals, Seventh Circuit
retrieval_notes: 538 F.3d 706 (7th Cir. 2008). Search Westlaw for US v. Calimlim. Domestic servitude case — Filipino domestic worker.
summary: Seventh Circuit affirmed convictions for forced labour and document servitude of a Filipino domestic worker held in servitude for over 19 years by a couple who confiscated her passport and threatened deportation. Important precedent on psychological coercion as a means of maintaining forced labour.
key_pinpoint: Pages 710-715 (psychological coercion as means), pages 716-720 (document servitude)

title: United States v. Kil Soo Lee, 472 F.3d 638 (9th Cir. 2006)
issuing_body: US Court of Appeals, Ninth Circuit
retrieval_notes: 472 F.3d 638 (9th Cir. 2006). Search Westlaw for US v. Kil Soo Lee. Major forced labour case — Samoan garment factory.
summary: Ninth Circuit affirmed convictions for involuntary servitude and forced labour of over 200 Vietnamese and Chinese workers in a Samoan garment factory. Workers were confined, beaten, and denied food. Key authority on the distinction between forced labour and voluntary employment under coercive conditions.
key_pinpoint: Section III (elements of forced labour under 18 USC 1589), Section IV (sufficiency of evidence for involuntary servitude)

title: United States v. Kozminski, 487 U.S. 931 (1988)
issuing_body: Supreme Court of the United States
retrieval_notes: 487 U.S. 931 (1988). Available on Westlaw and LexisNexis. Also check CourtListener (Free Law Project) — older SCOTUS opinions are sometimes available. Export full opinion.
summary: US Supreme Court case defining "involuntary servitude" under 18 USC § 1584, narrowing it to compulsion through physical or legal coercion. Key to understanding the threshold for identifying victims of forced labour under pre-TVPA federal law, and still cited in trafficking cases on the meaning of coercion for victim identification purposes.
key_pinpoint: 487 U.S. at 944–952 (definition of involuntary servitude and coercion)

title: UNODC Model Law against Trafficking in Persons (2009)
issuing_body: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime
summary: Model legislation to assist states in implementing the Trafficking Protocol. Provides template provisions covering criminalisation of trafficking (elements of offence), victim protection and assistance, prevention measures, and international cooperation. Key reference for comparing national implementations.
key_pinpoint: Chapter II (criminalisation — act, means, purpose elements), Chapter III (victim protection), Chapter V (international cooperation)

title: Brazil: Lei 13.344/2016 (Lei de Enfrentamento ao Tráfico de Pessoas)
summary: Brazilian anti-trafficking law replacing previous provisions. Defines trafficking as recruitment, transport, transfer, harbouring, or receipt for the purpose of exploitation. Covers sexual exploitation, forced labour, servitude, organ removal, and irregular adoption. Establishes prevention, protection, and prosecution framework.
key_pinpoint: Art. 2 (definition of trafficking), Art. 13 (offence — penalty 4-8 years), Art. 7 (victim protection measures)

title: Canada: Criminal Code, ss.279.01-279.04 (Trafficking in Persons)
summary: Canadian criminal code provisions on trafficking in persons. S.279.01 criminalises trafficking (recruiting, transporting, harbouring, exercising control for exploitation) with maximum life imprisonment. S.279.04 defines exploitation as causing a person to provide labour or services through fear, deception, or abuse of power.
key_pinpoint: s.279.01 (trafficking offence), s.279.011 (trafficking of person under 18), s.279.02 (material benefit), s.279.04 (exploitation defined)

title: Canada: Immigration and Refugee Protection Act, s.118 (Trafficking in Persons)
summary: Immigration provision criminalising transnational trafficking into Canada. Prohibits knowingly organising entry of persons through abduction, fraud, deception, use of force, or coercion. Maximum penalty of life imprisonment and $1M fine. Complements Criminal Code trafficking provisions for cross-border cases.
key_pinpoint: s.118(1) (offence of trafficking — cross-border), s.118(2) (penalty — life/$1M), s.120.1 (minimum sentences)

title: France: Code Pénal, Articles 225-4-1 to 225-4-9 (Traite des êtres humains)
summary: French criminal code provisions on trafficking in human beings. Art. 225-4-1 defines trafficking as recruiting, transporting, transferring, harbouring, or receiving a person for exploitation using threats, coercion, fraud, or abuse of vulnerability. Penalties range from 7 to 20 years depending on aggravating factors.
key_pinpoint: Art. 225-4-1 (definition), Art. 225-4-2 (aggravating circumstances), Art. 225-4-9 (extra-territorial jurisdiction)

title: Netherlands: Wetboek van Strafrecht, Article 273f (Mensenhandel)
summary: Dutch criminal code provision on human trafficking (mensenhandel). Article 273f is the single comprehensive trafficking provision covering all forms of exploitation. Notably does not require proof of means for domestic trafficking of adults, and carries maximum penalties of 12-18 years.
key_pinpoint: Art. 273f(1)(1) (trafficking definition — no means required for domestic cases), sub-paragraphs 2-9 (specific exploitation forms)

title: Philippines: Republic Act 10364, Expanded Anti-Trafficking in Persons Act of 2012
summary: Amendments strengthening RA 9208. Expands definition of trafficking to cover online sexual exploitation, adds qualified trafficking offences (involving children, public officials), strengthens penalties, and enhances victim protection including confidentiality provisions and mandatory restitution.
key_pinpoint: Sec. 3 (expanded definitions — online exploitation), Sec. 4-A (qualified trafficking), Sec. 11-A (mandatory restitution)

title: Philippines: Republic Act 9208, Anti-Trafficking in Persons Act of 2003
summary: Philippine primary anti-trafficking legislation. Defines and criminalises trafficking in persons with penalties of 20 years to life imprisonment. Establishes the Inter-Agency Council Against Trafficking (IACAT) and provides for victim protection, recovery, and reintegration programmes.
key_pinpoint: Sec. 3 (definition of trafficking), Sec. 4 (acts of trafficking), Sec. 10 (penalties), Sec. 20 (IACAT creation)

title: Trafficking Survivors Relief Act 2026
summary: Most recent federal trafficking legislation signed into law. Provides post-conviction relief mechanisms for trafficking survivors convicted of offences committed as a result of their trafficking.

title: South Africa: Prevention and Combating of Trafficking in Persons Act 7 of 2013 (PACOTIP)
summary: South Africa's comprehensive anti-trafficking legislation. Criminalises trafficking in persons with penalties up to life imprisonment. Addresses all forms of exploitation including forced labour, sexual exploitation, servitude, and organ removal. Includes provisions for victim identification, protection, and the role of social workers.
key_pinpoint: s.4 (offence of trafficking), s.1 (definitions — exploitation), s.18-23 (victim identification and protection), s.13 (penalties)

title: Canada Department of Justice: Handbook for Criminal Justice Practitioners on Trafficking in Persons
summary: Canadian DOJ handbook for prosecutors, law enforcement, and other criminal justice practitioners on trafficking in persons. Covers the legal framework (Criminal Code and IRPA provisions), investigation techniques, prosecution strategies, victim considerations, and available sentences.
key_pinpoint: Chapter 3 (legal framework — s.279.01-279.04 Criminal Code), Chapter 4 (investigation), Chapter 5 (prosecution strategies), Chapter 6 (victim-centred approach)

title: ILO Forced Labour and Human Trafficking: Casebook of Court Decisions (2009)
summary: 120-page training manual for judges, prosecutors, legal practitioners. Examines forced labour and trafficking cases across international courts, regional courts, South Asia, Europe, Americas. Comparative law training resource.
key_pinpoint: Cases by region; comparative offence elements; judicial reasoning on forced labour across jurisdictions

title: UNODC Case Digest: Evidential Issues in Trafficking Cases
summary: Analysis of evidential issues from 135 cases across 31 jurisdictions. Covers: proving means element, establishing exploitation purpose, consent irrelevance, corroborating testimony, financial evidence, expert evidence on trauma.
key_pinpoint: Evidential patterns; corroboration approaches; financial evidence as exploitation proxy; expert witnesses on trauma

title: DOJ Human Trafficking Prosecution Unit (HTPU) Resources
summary: DOJ's HTPU within the Civil Rights Division provides federal prosecution guidance, coordinates multi-district investigations, and publishes best practices. Works with FBI, HSI, and US Attorneys' offices.
key_pinpoint: Prosecution of 18 USC §§1589–1592; coordination with FBI/HSI; restitution under §1593

title: ICC Office of the Prosecutor: Policy on Slavery Crimes (2024)
issuing_body: International Criminal Court, Office of the Prosecutor
summary: ICC OTP policy paper on the investigation and prosecution of slavery crimes under the Rome Statute. Sets out the Office's approach to enslavement, sexual slavery, forced labour, and trafficking as crimes against humanity and war crimes. Addresses the modern forms of slavery doctrine.
key_pinpoint: Section III (elements of slavery crimes under Rome Statute), Section IV (investigation methodology), Section V (victim participation)

title: Independent Anti-Slavery Commissioner (UK) — Section 45 Defence: Call for Evidence
summary: Critical report on practical application of §45 defence. Identifies widespread misunderstanding across law enforcement and CPS. Notes recording systems cannot track where §45 is raised. Highlights risk that misunderstanding creates 'blanket immunity' perception making victims attractive as couriers.
key_pinpoint: Three scenarios (pre-charge, discontinued, trial); operational failures in identification; recording recommendations

title: Modern Slavery PEC: Barriers to Prosecution of Modern Slavery Offences (2024)
issuing_body: Modern Slavery and Human Rights Policy and Evidence Centre
summary: Research report analysing barriers to effective prosecution of modern slavery offences in England and Wales. Covers: low prosecution rates relative to NRM referrals, evidential challenges, victim engagement difficulties, CPS charging decisions, and recommendations for improving prosecution outcomes.
key_pinpoint: Section 3 (evidential barriers), Section 4 (victim engagement challenges), Section 5 (CPS decision-making analysis), Section 6 (recommendations)

title: Modern Slavery PEC — Research & Resources
summary: UK independent research centre. Key publications include prosecution barriers analysis (Jun 2025: 467 CPS prosecutions, 355 convictions Jul 2023–Jun 2024), NRM data, ethical research guidance. Responds to House of Lords MSA Committee.
key_pinpoint: 2025 prosecution barriers report; NRM analysis; policy briefs for practitioners

title: Human Trafficking Institute — Federal Human Trafficking Report (Annual)
summary: Only exhaustive annual review of every federal criminal trafficking prosecution since 2000. Attorneys review every case via PACER, WestLaw, DOJ. Case-level data on demographics, methods, sentencing, by district. Interactive data at data.traffickinginstitute.org.
key_pinpoint: Exhaustive case database; interactive visualizations; state-by-state summaries; trends since 2000

title: CPS Guidance on Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking and Smuggling
summary: Comprehensive CPS prosecution guidance covering modern slavery and trafficking offences, the s. 45 statutory defence for victims, and prosecutorial obligations to identify potential trafficking victims before charging. Requires early investigative advice in all modern slavery cases and sets out the framework for distinguishing smuggling from trafficking.
key_pinpoint: Sections on s. 45 defence, duty to obtain early investigative advice, victim identification indicators, and NRM referral obligations

title: CPS Legal Guidance: Non-Prosecution of Victims of Trafficking
summary: CPS practice guidance implementing the non-punishment principle for trafficking victims. Directs prosecutors to identify potential trafficking victims before making prosecution decisions, applying public interest considerations and the s. 45 defence. Requires that where a credible defence of trafficking-compelled offending exists, the case should not be charged or should be discontinued.
key_pinpoint: Sections on non-punishment principle, public interest test for trafficking victims, duress and s. 45 defence assessment, and CPS duty to investigate trafficking indicators pre-charge

title: Europol European Migrant Smuggling Centre (EMSC) — 9th Annual Report (2025)
summary: Annual Europol report on migrant smuggling trends and the overlap with trafficking in human beings. Covers key routes, criminal networks, and the exploitation of migrants in transit and at destination. Relevant to investigations into the trafficking-smuggling nexus.
key_pinpoint: Sections on trafficking-smuggling nexus, key routes and criminal networks

title: EU Serious and Organised Crime Threat Assessment (EU-SOCTA) 2025
summary: Europol strategic threat assessment on serious and organised crime in the EU. Trafficking in human beings chapter covers criminal networks, routes, exploitation types, use of technology, and the nexus between trafficking and other crime types.
key_pinpoint: THB chapter (criminal networks, routes, exploitation types), section on technology-facilitated trafficking

title: FATF: Financial Flows from Human Trafficking (2018)
summary: FATF report analysing financial flows associated with human trafficking. Identifies typologies of money laundering linked to trafficking, red flag indicators for financial institutions, and recommendations for strengthening financial investigation in trafficking cases.
key_pinpoint: Section 3 (financial flow typologies), Section 4 (red flag indicators), Section 5 (recommendations for financial institutions)

title: FATF: Money Laundering Risks Arising from Trafficking in Human Beings and Smuggling of Migrants (2011)
summary: Early FATF report on money laundering risks associated with human trafficking and migrant smuggling. Establishes the foundation for financial investigation approaches in trafficking cases, including use of financial intelligence to identify trafficking networks.
key_pinpoint: Section on ML typologies, section on financial intelligence sharing, recommendations for FIUs

title: UNODC Anti-Human Trafficking Manual — Module 5.9: Crime Scene Investigation
issuing_body: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime
summary: UNODC guidance on crime scene investigation in trafficking cases. Covers evidence collection from trafficking premises (brothels, factories, farms), digital evidence, forensic evidence, and chain of custody requirements specific to trafficking investigations.
key_pinpoint: Module 5.9 (crime scene protocols), digital evidence section, forensic evidence collection guidance

title: ILO-IOE Combating Forced Labour: Handbook for Employers (3rd Ed, 2025)
summary: Third edition (Nov 2025). Uses ILO 11 indicators to help businesses identify forced labour risks. Covers due diligence, remediation, multi-stakeholder collaboration. For management, HR, procurement, legal, compliance.
key_pinpoint: Risk assessment using 11 indicators; remediation framework; grievance mechanism design

title: College of Policing: Modern Slavery Investigation Guidance
summary: Operational guidance for UK police. Covers initial response, victim engagement, evidence gathering, financial investigation, organised crime approaches, and international cooperation.
key_pinpoint: Proactive intelligence-led operations; victim-less prosecution strategies; financial investigation

title: NCA Strategic Assessment 2025 — Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Chapter
summary: NCA annual strategic assessment of the modern slavery and human trafficking threat in the UK. Covers threat picture, criminal exploitation, county lines, labour exploitation in key sectors (car washes, nail bars, agriculture), and emerging trends including online facilitation.
key_pinpoint: MSHT chapter (threat assessment), key sectors, emerging trends

title: Krachunova v Bulgaria — Compensation as Article 4 Obligation (ECtHR, 2023)
summary: ECtHR landmark establishing that the positive obligation under Article 4 ECHR includes ensuring trafficking victims have effective access to compensation. Bulgaria failed to provide adequate mechanisms for the applicant to obtain compensation from her traffickers, violating Article 4.
key_pinpoint: Paras 145-165 (compensation as component of Art. 4 positive obligation), paras 166-180 (state failures in compensation mechanisms)

title: Krachunova v Bulgaria, App. No. 18269/18 (ECtHR, 2023)
summary: ECtHR held that Article 4 ECHR entails a positive obligation to provide effective access to compensation for trafficking victims. Bulgaria's failure to enable the applicant to obtain compensation from her traffickers violated her rights. Landmark on the right to compensation as an element of Art. 4 protection.
key_pinpoint: Paras 145-165 (compensation as component of Art. 4 positive obligation), paras 166-180 (state duty to facilitate compensation)

title: Galdikas & Others v DJ Houghton Catching Services Ltd [2016]
retrieval_notes: Search BAILII/Westlaw for Galdikas v DJ Houghton Catching Services Ltd [2016] EWHC (QB). Civil claim by Lithuanian workers for forced labour in poultry catching.
summary: High Court civil claim by Lithuanian agricultural workers subjected to forced labour conditions in poultry catching. Established that civil remedies are available for trafficking victims under common law (breach of contract, false imprisonment) alongside statutory MSA remedies.
key_pinpoint: Judgment on liability (forced labour conditions), quantum of damages

title: Hounga v Allen [2014] UKSC 47
summary: Supreme Court held that the defence of illegality (ex turpi causa) could not bar a discrimination claim by a trafficked domestic worker, even where the worker had entered the UK illegally. The public policy against trafficking outweighed the public policy against aiding illegal immigration.
key_pinpoint: Paras 42-52 (illegality defence inapplicable to trafficking victims), para 60 (policy rationale)

title: A.B. v. Marriott International Inc., No. 2:19-cv-01738 (E.D. Pa. 2020)
issuing_body: US District Court, Eastern District of Pennsylvania
retrieval_notes: Case No. 2:19-cv-01738 (E.D. Pa.). Search PACER for A.B. v. Marriott International. TVPA beneficiary liability — hotel chain sex trafficking.
summary: Civil TVPA action against major hotel chains alleging they knowingly benefited from sex trafficking on their premises. Court denied motions to dismiss, holding that hotel operators can be liable under 18 USC 1595(a) if they knew or should have known trafficking was occurring and benefited from it.
key_pinpoint: Motion to dismiss ruling (knowledge standard under 18 USC 1595(a), beneficiary liability)

title: David v. Signal International LLC, No. 08-1220 (E.D. La. 2015)
issuing_body: US District Court, Eastern District of Louisiana
retrieval_notes: Case No. 1:08-cv-01220 (E.D. La.). Search PACER for David v. Signal International. Landmark TVPA civil remedy case — Indian H-2B guest workers.
summary: Landmark civil TVPA case. Indian guest workers recruited through fraud and held in labour trafficking conditions on oil rigs after Hurricane Katrina. Jury awarded $14M in damages under 18 USC 1595. Established that recruitment fraud in the home country constitutes trafficking under the TVPA.
key_pinpoint: Jury verdict (TVPA civil liability for recruitment fraud), damages award under 18 USC 1595

title: Doe v. Apple Inc. (D.C. Cir. 2024)
issuing_body: US Court of Appeals, D.C. Circuit
summary: DRC cobalt miners sued Apple, Alphabet, Microsoft, Dell, Tesla for benefiting from child/forced labor. D.C. Circuit affirmed dismissal — TVPRA civil remedy (18 USC §1595) does not apply extraterritorially. Important limitation on supply chain litigation.
key_pinpoint: §1595 lacks extraterritorial reach; implications for supply chain accountability

title: Council Directive 2004/80/EC Relating to Compensation to Crime Victims
summary: EU directive requiring Member States to ensure that compensation is available to victims of violent intentional crime committed in their territory, including trafficking victims. Establishes a system of cooperation between Member States for cross-border compensation claims.
key_pinpoint: Art. 1 (right to compensation), Art. 12 (cross-border cooperation), Art. 2 (national compensation schemes)

title: Directive (EU) 2024/1712 Amending Directive 2011/36/EU on Preventing and Combating Trafficking in Human Beings
summary: Amending directive strengthening the EU anti-trafficking framework. Adds forced marriage, illegal adoption, and forced surrogacy as forms of exploitation. Strengthens compensation provisions by requiring Member States to ensure trafficking victims have effective access to compensation, including from state funds.
key_pinpoint: Art. 1(7) (new Art. 17 — compensation), Art. 1(3) (expanded exploitation definitions), Art. 1(5) (demand reduction)

title: UK MSA 2015 — Slavery and Trafficking Reparation Orders (§8–§10)
summary: §8 empowers courts to make reparation orders requiring convicted offenders to pay compensation from confiscated assets under POCA 2002. Court must consider making an order in every conviction with a confiscation order.
key_pinpoint: §8 (power); §9 (duty to consider where confiscation order made); §10 (enforcement)

title: 18 U.S.C. § 1595 — Civil Remedy for Trafficking Victims
summary: Federal civil remedy provision allowing trafficking victims to bring civil actions against their traffickers and any person or entity that knowingly benefited from trafficking. Provides for actual damages, punitive damages, and attorneys' fees. Also permits state attorneys general to bring civil actions.
key_pinpoint: § 1595(a) (private right of action), § 1595(d) (statute of limitations — 10 years), § 1595(a) (beneficiary liability standard)

title: Mandatory Restitution (18 USC §1593) & Crime Victims' Rights Act (§3771)
summary: §1593 mandates full restitution in federal trafficking cases — court SHALL order for full amount of losses. §3771 provides broad victims' rights including right to restitution, right to be heard at sentencing, and right to proceedings free from unreasonable delay.
key_pinpoint: §1593(b) (restitution = full value of labor + legal costs + other losses); §3771(a)(6) (right to full and timely restitution)

title: Criminal Injuries Compensation Authority (CICA) — Application Guidance for Trafficking Victims
summary: Guidance on how victims of modern slavery and trafficking can apply to the CICA scheme. Covers eligibility criteria (crime of violence requirement), time limits, evidence needed, and how trafficking-specific factors (e.g., delayed reporting, immigration status) are treated in claims.
key_pinpoint: Eligibility criteria for trafficking victims, time limit exceptions, evidence requirements

title: Human Trafficking Legal Center — Civil & Criminal Case Databases
summary: Comprehensive databases of civil and criminal federal trafficking cases. Civil database tracks all §1595 litigation since 2003. Also publishes extraterritorial jurisdiction guides (France, Switzerland). Pro bono lawyer matching for survivors.
key_pinpoint: Civil database (929 cases, >$900M); criminal database; extraterritorial guides; pro bono network

title: HTLC — Federal Civil Trafficking Litigation Report (2003–2023)
summary: All federal civil trafficking cases under 18 USC §1595 from 2003–2023. 929 cases, >$900M damages. Tracks geographic reach (48 states), case types (77% include forced labor), outcomes, and damages awards.
key_pinpoint: Case outcome statistics; damages quantum; geographic distribution; forced labour vs sex trafficking breakdown

title: Eurojust — Joint Investigation Teams in Trafficking Cases
summary: Eurojust facilitates judicial cooperation in cross-border trafficking: JIT formation, funding, European Arrest Warrants, European Investigation Orders. Practical model from UK-Slovakia cannabis/labour exploitation case.
key_pinpoint: JIT formation; EAW and EIO execution; coordination centre model; evidence sharing

title: Eurojust Report on Prosecuting THB for the Purpose of Labour Exploitation
summary: Practical guidance for prosecutors and judicial authorities on investigating and prosecuting trafficking for labour exploitation. Analyses judgments and Eurojust casework to identify challenges in proving the labour exploitation element, and provides recommendations on victim identification, evidence gathering, and cross-border cooperation through Joint Investigation Teams.

title: Eurojust Report on Trafficking in Human Beings
summary: Comprehensive analysis of Eurojust's casework on trafficking in human beings between 2017 and 2020, examining 91 THB cases and providing 18 targeted recommendations for prosecutors, judges, and law enforcement. Addresses judicial coordination challenges, victim identification across borders, and the importance of early identification in cross-border investigations.
key_pinpoint: Recommendations 1–18; Part II on victims' rights

title: R v Iyamu [2018] EWCA Crim 2166
summary: Court of Appeal upheld 18-year sentence for conspiracy to arrange travel for sexual exploitation. Iyamu recruited Nigerian women through juju rituals and arranged their transport to Europe. Key sentencing authority on culpability factors in trafficking for sexual exploitation.
key_pinpoint: Paras 30-45 (sentencing principles for trafficking offences), paras 46-55 (aggravating factors: juju, multiple victims)

title: R v Iyamu — Sentencing Remarks [2017] (Central Criminal Court)
retrieval_notes: Search BAILII/Westlaw for R v Iyamu sentencing remarks 2017. Trial judge's sentencing remarks at the Old Bailey. The appeal is at [2018] EWCA Crim 2166.
summary: Trial judge's sentencing remarks imposing 18 years for conspiracy to arrange travel for sexual exploitation of Nigerian women through juju rituals. Detailed analysis of culpability and harm factors, including exploitation of vulnerability, multiple victims, and sustained offending.
key_pinpoint: Culpability assessment (category A — leading role, coercion through juju), harm assessment (multiple vulnerable victims)

title: United States Sentencing Guidelines § 2H4.1 — Peonage, Involuntary Servitude, Forced Labor, Trafficking in Persons
summary: Federal sentencing guidelines for trafficking offences under 18 USC 1581-1592. Base offence level 22 with specific offence characteristics increasing levels for: number of victims, use of threats/violence, victims under 18, commercial sex acts, and death resulting. Key US federal sentencing framework.
key_pinpoint: § 2H4.1(b) (specific offence characteristics), Application Notes (definitions of victim, commercial sex act)

title: Sentencing Council: Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking and Slavery, Servitude and Forced or Compulsory Labour — Definitive Guideline (2021)
issuing_body: Sentencing Council for England and Wales
summary: Definitive sentencing guideline for MSA 2015 offences in England and Wales. Sets out culpability factors (A-C), harm categories (1-3), aggravating/mitigating factors, and starting points. Key resource for prosecutors and judges on sentencing modern slavery offences.
key_pinpoint: Step 1 (culpability categories), Step 2 (harm categories and starting points), aggravating factors (vulnerability of victims, financial gain)

title: UNODC Anti-Human Trafficking Manual — Module 5.17: Witness Protection Principles
issuing_body: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime
summary: UNODC guidance on witness protection principles for trafficking cases. Covers threat assessment, formal witness protection programmes, alternative protection measures, cross-border witness relocation, and the balance between witness safety and fair trial rights of the accused.
key_pinpoint: Module 5.17 (threat assessment criteria), protection measures hierarchy, cross-border relocation protocols

title: UNODC Anti-Human Trafficking Manual — Module 5.18: Witness Protection During Trial
issuing_body: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime
summary: UNODC guidance on protecting witnesses during trafficking trials. Covers courtroom protective measures (screens, closed sessions, remote testimony), anonymity orders, prevention of secondary traumatisation, and practical measures for witness comfort and safety during proceedings.
key_pinpoint: Module 5.18 (courtroom measures), anonymity orders, remote testimony procedures, preventing secondary traumatisation

title: Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999, ss.16-33 (Special Measures)
summary: Primary legislation governing special measures for vulnerable and intimidated witnesses in criminal proceedings. Includes screens, live links, evidence given in private, video-recorded evidence-in-chief, and intermediaries. Section 28 enables pre-recorded cross-examination for vulnerable witnesses.
key_pinpoint: s.16 (eligible witnesses — age/incapacity), s.17 (eligible witnesses — fear/distress), ss.23-30 (specific special measures), s.28 (pre-recorded cross-examination)

title: CPS Guidance on Special Measures
summary: CPS guidance for prosecutors on identifying eligible witnesses and applying for special measures under the YJCEA 1999. Covers assessment of vulnerability and intimidation, available measures, application procedure, and specific considerations for trafficking victims.
key_pinpoint: Section on eligible witnesses, section on available measures, specific guidance for trafficking/exploitation cases

title: Criminal Practice Directions 2023, Division 18E — Pre-Recorded Cross-Examination (Section 28)
issuing_body: Lord Chief Justice of England and Wales
summary: Practice directions governing the operation of s.28 YJCEA 1999 pre-recorded cross-examination for vulnerable witnesses. Sets out eligibility, procedure for ground rules hearings, recording requirements, and restrictions on questioning. Now rolled out to all Crown Courts.
key_pinpoint: CPD 18E.1-18E.12 (procedure for s.28), ground rules hearings, restrictions on questioning

title: Equal Treatment Bench Book (Judicial College)
summary: Comprehensive guide for judges on ensuring fair treatment in court for persons with protected characteristics and vulnerabilities. Chapter on trafficking and modern slavery covers trauma responses, cultural factors, and practical adjustments. Chapter on vulnerable witnesses covers communication needs and intermediaries.
key_pinpoint: Chapter 11 (Human Trafficking and Modern Slavery), Chapter 2 (Disabilities — trauma), Chapter 6 (Vulnerable witnesses)

title: DOJ National Strategy to Combat Human Trafficking (2022)
summary: DOJ comprehensive strategy addressing victim-centred and trauma-informed approaches to human trafficking prosecution. Includes commitments on training for federal prosecutors and judges, improving courtroom practices for trafficking victims, and expanding victim services coordination.
key_pinpoint: Goal 2 (victim-centred prosecution), Goal 3 (training on trauma-informed approaches), Goal 4 (interagency coordination)

title: US State Department: Trauma-Informed Approach to Human Trafficking (2019)
issuing_body: US Department of State, Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons
summary: US State Department guidance on applying trauma-informed principles to human trafficking response. Explains how trauma affects trafficking victims' behaviour, memory, and engagement with the justice system, and how practitioners can adapt their approaches accordingly.
key_pinpoint: Section on trauma impacts (memory, behaviour, trust), section on practical adaptations for law enforcement and courts
